food_subgroup: Marinated pork dish with rice and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork bulgogi dosirak; closest parent 'pork dish' (FOODON_00002725) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (pork meat dish) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for bulgogi dosirak exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pork bulgogi dosirak or Korean lunchbox meal.

main_ingredients: bulgogi marinade (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, pear or apple)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard pork bulgogi dosirak set weighing approximately 550 g, comprising marinated stir-fried pork, steamed white rice, two pork-and-chive mandu, and two banchan side dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Bulgogi (pork), cooked — Korean composite dish nutrient profile; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central 'Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised' and HPB Singapore nutrient data for Korean-style rice sets
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Pork bulgogi dosirak is a composite meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 35% steamed white rice (~130 kcal/100 g), 40% marinated cooked pork bulgogi (~200 kcal/100 g), 10% mandu (~220 kcal/100 g), 15% banchan (~30 kcal/100 g). Weighted average yields approximately 162 kcal per 100 g of the combined meal. Sodium is elevated due to soy-sauce-based marinade and banchan. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pork loin and steamed white rice, and HPB Singapore data for Korean rice sets. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (boiled) and mixed Korean rice-based meals
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork bulgogi dosirak as a composite meal. GI estimated at 58 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (GI ~64 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr). The protein and fat content of the pork bulgogi and mandu components attenuate the overall meal GI. A composite meal GI of approximately 58 (medium) is defensible per published mixed-meal GI methodology (Wolever & Jenkins, 1986). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 107.25 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving at 19.5 g/100 g) / 100 ≈ 20.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce-based bulgogi marinade and banchan side dishes
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single 550 g serving provides approximately 2,640 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, partially attenuated by protein and fat from pork
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the medium GI (approximately 58) and glycemic load of approximately 20.8 per serving can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion would lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from pork and elevated sodium from marinade and banchan
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should be aware that a full serving contributes approximately 9.4 g saturated fat and 2,640 mg sodium, both of which exceed recommended single-meal limits; choosing leaner pork cuts and lower-sodium banchan would improve the cardiovascular profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Bulgogi Dosirak (돼지불고기 도시락) is a Korean lunchbox meal consisting of thinly sliced pork marinated in a classic bulgogi sauce — typically a blend of soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and grated Asian pear or apple for tenderisation — that is then stir-fried at high heat in a wok to achieve a caramelised, slightly smoky flavour. The dosirak format (도시락, meaning lunchbox) assembles the marinated pork alongside a generous portion of steamed white rice, crispy pork-and-chive mandu (pan-fried or steamed dumplings), and two banchan (Korean side dishes) such as kimchi, seasoned spinach, or pickled radish. The pork is tender and juicy with a sweet-savoury umami profile, and the mandu add a crispy textural contrast. Nutritionally, the meal is a moderate-calorie, protein-rich composite dish providing approximately 162 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g) from pork and mandu, and carbohydrate (~19.5 g/100 g) primarily from white rice. Sodium content is notably high (~480 mg/100 g) due to the soy-sauce marinade and banchan, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), driven by the white rice component but partially attenuated by the protein and fat from pork. This dish is a popular Korean comfort food widely served in Korean restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork bulgogi dosirak?
What is in a Korean lunchbox?
Is pork bulgogi dosirak high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat pork bulgogi dosirak?

confidence_reason: Pork bulgogi dosirak is a composite restaurant meal not directly listed in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pork loin and steamed white rice, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for Korean rice sets. GI is estimated from the analogous white rice GI in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite meal with estimated rather than directly measured nutrient values.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model (35% steamed white rice, 40% marinated cooked pork bulgogi, 10% mandu, 15% banchan) and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed. 2. Sodium content is likely to vary significantly between restaurants depending on marinade recipe and banchan selection. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 8. The halal status is listed as false because pork is not halal; this dish is not suitable for halal-observant consumers. 9. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component (GI 64, University of Sydney GI Database) attenuated by the protein and fat content of the composite meal; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in approved sources. 10. Serving size of 550 g is estimated based on typical Korean dosirak set meal weights; actual restaurant portion may vary.
